subject: Astronomy & Space
title: SN 1987A: The Supernova We Watched
description: Learn SN 1987A: The Supernova We Watched in one minute.

hook.text: On February 23, 1987, a star exploded in a nearby galaxy. It was the closest supernova in 400 years.

buildup.text: Astronomers detected neutrinos from the blast hours before the light arrived—confirming supernova theory.

discovery.text: The exploding star was Sanduleak −69° 202, a blue supergiant. Theorists had predicted only red supergiants could blow.

twist.text: The neutrino burst lasted 13 seconds but carried 99% of the explosion's energy. Light carried almost none.

climax.text: Decades later, dust is forming in the debris. A new generation of planets may someday emerge from the wreckage.

punchline.text: We watched a star die in real time. It rewrote the textbooks.

quiz.question: What surprised scientists about SN 1987A?
options: A blue supergiant exploded, not a red one as theory predicted
It produced no visible light at all
It occurred in our own Milky Way galaxy
